courses.title: Level Up
courses.description: Skills, integrations, and automated workflows that turn ChatGPT into your operating system.

lessons.title: Skills That Compound
lessons.description: Install add-on abilities that stack together and multiply your output.

sections.content:
Skills are add-on abilities that extend what ChatGPT can do. They come in three tiers, and understanding the distinction between them is what separates people who get marginal improvement from people who fundamentally change how they work.

Tier 1: Custom Instructions. Simple behavior tweaks that persist across conversations — your role, preferred response format, things ChatGPT should always or never do. These are free, take two minutes to set up in Settings → Personalization, and apply to every interaction. If you haven't configured Custom Instructions yet, this is your highest-leverage first move.

Tier 2: Custom GPTs from the GPT Store. Pre-built workflows for specific professional use cases — brand voice engines, code review checklists, research summaries, writing assistants. These exist because common workflows have been packaged into one-click installs. The best ones understand your context and pull from curated knowledge sources.

Tier 3: Custom GPTs you build yourself. GPTs you design in the GPT Builder with tailored instructions, uploaded knowledge, and custom actions that connect to APIs and external tools. The highest leverage — but requires the most setup investment.

One skill is a convenience. Five skills working together can fundamentally change how you operate. A drafting Custom GPT that knows your voice, integrated with real client data, means ChatGPT can write a personalized follow-up without you providing any details manually. That's not a better chatbot — that's a different category of tool.

How to think about the installation decision: Before you install or build anything, ask what you're actually paying. Skills aren't always free — they cost setup time, mental overhead, and sometimes subscription fees. The question isn't whether a skill sounds useful. The question is whether it saves you more time than it costs to configure and maintain. A Custom GPT that takes 3 hours to set up and saves you 5 minutes per week takes over a year to pay for itself. One that takes 30 minutes and saves you 30 minutes daily pays back in two days.

How to evaluate whether a skill is worth installing:

The critical question: does this Custom GPT give you something you couldn't get by just typing a prompt to ChatGPT directly? If the answer is no — if the GPT is just a fancier way to submit a prompt — skip it. You're adding complexity without adding capability.

A skill that's worth installing does at least one of these:

  • Reads your actual context — files you've uploaded, data you've connected — rather than just accepting what you paste in
  • Maintains consistency automatically — brand voice, coding standards, output format — so you don't have to specify it each time
  • Runs a structured workflow — multiple steps in sequence — rather than just wrapping a prompt
  • Integrates with an external system — via custom actions connecting to your database, email, or project management tool

A skill that fails these tests is a gimmick. It feels useful when you install it and gets ignored within a week. The habits that compound over time are the ones that save you real steps on real tasks you do repeatedly.

Evaluating a Custom GPT in 60 seconds: Before installing, answer: What specific task does this handle? Does it need my data to work, or does it generate generic output? Can I describe the exact workflow it saves me? If you can't answer all three concretely, keep evaluating.

The context-awareness test: Here's a quick filter. Imagine running the GPT on a task you did last week. Does it actually know the details it needs — your client's name, your project's status, your company's standards — or does it just produce something plausible-sounding? A GPT that knows your context produces work you could send. One that doesn't produces work you have to rewrite. Only the first one is worth the install.

Red flags to watch for: A Custom GPT that claims to do everything is usually good at nothing. GPTs that require you to paste in all your data before they start are often just prompt wrappers with extra steps. GPTs without clear documentation about what knowledge they have are risks. If you can't tell whether a GPT is reading your uploaded files or generating from thin air, assume the latter until proven otherwise.

The compounding effect — why your skill stack matters more than any single skill:

Skills compound when they connect to each other. The marketing team example makes this concrete: a brand voice Custom GPT alone produces on-brand content. A content calendar GPT alone produces topic ideas. A drafting GPT alone produces generic posts. Stack all three — calendar decides what to write, drafting generates it, brand voice enforces consistency — and you have a production system that runs in a fraction of the time with better output than any of the pieces could produce alone.

The compounding works because each skill feeds its output to the next. This is why the order of installation matters: foundation skills first, then generation skills on top, then planning skills that direct the generation. Get the order wrong and you're adding complexity without multiplying capability.

A real stack that works: Consider how a solo consultant might build this. First, Custom Instructions that establish her role, preferred output format, and communication style. Second, a Custom GPT loaded with her proposal templates and deliverable formats. Third, a research GPT configured for her industry. Each GPT added to this stack multiplies the value of the ones already there. The proposal GPT is dramatically more useful when ChatGPT already knows her communication style from Custom Instructions. Compounding.

The investment mindset: Skills take time to set up and configure properly. The people who resist this upfront investment stay stuck at the same output level indefinitely. The people who invest a few hours in their skill stack — Custom Instructions, two or three GPT Store installs, one custom-built GPT for their most frequent workflow — get that time back within the first week and keep getting it back every week after.

Quarterly skill audits: Not all skills age well. A Custom GPT you installed six months ago might overlap with something new in the GPT Store. Custom Instructions that were helpful in one role might be wrong now that your focus has shifted. Build a quarterly habit: review your installed GPTs, uninstall what you're not using, and upgrade anything that's become outdated. An optimized stack of five active GPTs beats a cluttered list of twenty installed-and-forgotten ones.

The two-minute test for any new skill: Before installing anything, run it through this test. Open a real task from your actual work — something you did last week, not a hypothetical. Ask: would this GPT have saved me meaningful time on this specific task? If yes, install it. If the answer is "maybe" or "possibly," wait. Install GPTs when you have a concrete, recent example of the workflow they handle. Concrete examples beat general optimism every time.

Skills come in three tiers: Custom Instructions (behavior tweaks), GPT Store installs (pre-built workflows), and Custom GPTs you build (your own automations). Evaluate skills by context-awareness and real workflow impact — not feature lists. Use the 60-second filter: specific task, needs your data, saves a defined workflow. Stack skills in the right order: foundation, then generation, then planning. The compounding effect means three connected skills multiply output in ways that no single skill can match. Audit your stack quarterly — remove what's unused, upgrade what's stale. Invest in setup now; it pays dividends on every future interaction.

exercises.prompt: You want ChatGPT to always assume you're a senior developer, default to bullet-point responses, and use your team's coding standards. Figure out the right skill approach.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Choosing the Right Skill Tier", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You have three types of skills available: Custom Instructions (simple behavior tweaks), GPT Store Custom GPTs (pre-built solutions), and Custom GPTs you build yourself. Each fits different needs."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You want ChatGPT to always know your role, preferred response format, and coding standards. These are persistent behavior preferences.", "question": "Which skill tier fits this need?", "options": ["A GPT Store install -- search for 'senior developer mode'", "Custom Instructions -- simple behavior tweaks that persist across conversations", "Build a custom GPT in GPT Builder", "No skill needed -- just explain it each time"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Custom Instructions are perfect for persistent preferences: role, format, standards. Lightweight, no install, shape every response. Custom GPTs are overkill here."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Now your team wants a standardized PR review process that checks 5 specific things, reads actual code files, and produces a formatted report.", "question": "Which skill tier fits THIS need?", "options": ["Custom Instructions -- just add more text to the instructions", "A GPT Store code review GPT configured with your team's criteria", "Write it on a sticky note and check manually"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "This is a structured, repeated workflow -- exactly what Custom GPTs handle. Custom Instructions would be too lightweight for a multi-step process that reads files and produces reports."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "The GPT Store review tool handles 4 of your 5 checks well, but the security check isn't specific enough for your codebase. Write a prompt that layers a custom security check on top of the existing GPT.", "placeholder": "Write your custom security addition...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["SQL", "injection", "concatenation", "parameterized", "query"], "label": "Mentioned SQL injection prevention", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["input", "validate", "sanitize", "user input", "unvalidated"], "label": "Referenced input validation", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["secret", "hardcoded", "API key", "password", "credential"], "label": "Included hardcoded secrets check", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["auth", "middleware", "authorization", "authentication"], "label": "Referenced auth checks", "points": 1}], "ideal": "After running the standard review, also check for: raw SQL concatenation (must use parameterized queries), unvalidated user input in API routes, missing auth middleware on protected endpoints, and hardcoded secrets or API keys.", "max_points": 6, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Layer custom additions on top of GPT Store installs. Don't rebuild what works -- extend it with your specific needs.
hints: Think about which checks are universal (GPT Store) vs. specific to your codebase (custom).

exercises.prompt: Two email Custom GPTs in the GPT Store. GPT A: 'Generates professional emails from a topic.' GPT B: 'Matches your tone from past emails, follows your company's communication guidelines, and suggests subject lines optimized for your audience.' Which is the better investment?
options: GPT A -- simpler is better, fewer things to break
GPT B -- it understands your full context, not just the task
Both are equal -- they both write emails
exercises.explanation: GPT B understands your context: your voice, your standards, and your audience. GPT A is a prompt wrapper -- it does the same thing as typing 'write an email about X' into ChatGPT directly. Context-aware Custom GPTs save workflows, not just steps.
hints: Which GPT gives you something you couldn't get by just asking ChatGPT directly?

exercises.prompt: Walk through evaluating and selecting the right skills for a marketing team's weekly content workflow.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Building a Skill Stack", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "A marketing team creates 3 LinkedIn posts and 1 newsletter per week. They use ChatGPT manually today -- pasting briefs, writing from scratch each time. You want to build a skill stack that handles ideation through final draft."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "You browse the GPT Store and find: 'Content Writer Pro' (generates content from topics), 'Brand Voice Engine' (reads your past content and maintains tone consistency), and 'Content Calendar AI' (plans topics based on audience analytics).", "question": "Which skill do you install first?", "options": ["Content Writer Pro -- it handles the core writing task", "Brand Voice Engine -- consistent voice is the foundation everything else builds on", "Content Calendar AI -- planning before writing", "Install all three at once"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Voice consistency is the foundation. Without it, every piece sounds different. Install Brand Voice Engine first, train it on your best content, then layer writing and planning GPTs on top."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Brand Voice Engine is configured and producing consistent tone. Content Writer Pro generates posts but they're generic -- hitting the right voice but not the right substance.", "question": "What's missing from the stack?", "options": ["Nothing -- just write better prompts", "The Content Calendar AI GPT -- it provides topic direction and audience relevance that the writer needs as input", "Replace Content Writer Pro with a different writing GPT"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "The writer has voice but no direction. Content Calendar AI feeds topic ideas based on audience engagement. Stack: Calendar (what) -> Writer (generate) -> Voice Engine (consistency). Each skill feeds the next."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "Your skill stack works for LinkedIn posts but the newsletter always needs heavy editing. The tone is right but the structure is wrong. Write a prompt to add newsletter-specific instructions.", "placeholder": "Write your newsletter structure instructions...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["newsletter", "email", "subject line", "header"], "label": "Referenced newsletter format", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["section", "structure", "intro", "body", "closing", "CTA", "template"], "label": "Defined structure/sections", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["length", "word", "short", "concise", "under"], "label": "Specified length constraints", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["link", "resource", "read more", "reference"], "label": "Mentioned supporting links", "points": 1}], "ideal": "For newsletters: Subject line under 50 chars that creates curiosity. Intro paragraph: 2 sentences, personal observation. 3 sections with headers. Each section: insight + practical takeaway + link to deeper resource. Closing: 1 question that prompts replies. Total under 500 words.", "max_points": 5, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Build skill stacks in layers: foundation (voice) first, then generation, then planning. When one output type needs fixing, add format-specific instructions rather than replacing the whole stack.
hints: What needs to be consistent before anything else matters? Start there.

lessons.title: Build and Share Custom GPTs
lessons.description: Create Custom GPTs and publish them for your team or the world.

sections.content:
The distinction between the three ChatGPT tiers isn't just about features — it's about what kind of skills you can build at each level.

With Custom Instructions, skills are behavior rules and saved preferences. You set them once in Settings and they apply to every conversation. These are lightweight but effective: your role, your preferred format, constraints that always apply. The limit is that they're just text instructions — they can't run structured workflows or integrate external tools.

In the GPT Store, you get marketplace Custom GPTs — pre-built capabilities that install in seconds. These are substantially more powerful because they execute structured workflows with curated knowledge, files, and in some cases custom actions that connect to external services.

In GPT Builder, you build Custom GPTs from scratch: tailored system instructions, uploaded knowledge files, DALL-E image generation, Code Interpreter, Browse with Bing, and custom actions that call external APIs. A Custom GPT that runs your proposal process, knows your templates, and connects to your project management tool. Or a customer service GPT that reads your product docs and handles tier-one inquiries. These are the most powerful skills, and you can share them.

The progression is natural: start with Custom Instructions (free, immediate), graduate to GPT Store installs (structured workflows for common use cases), and build your own Custom GPTs when your specific workflow doesn't match any marketplace option.

When building your own Custom GPT becomes the right call: If you find yourself running the same multi-step workflow three or more times a week — and no GPT Store option covers it — it's time to build. The threshold is roughly this: if you can describe every step in plain language and it involves consistent knowledge, format, or process, it's automatable. And if it's automatable and you're doing it repeatedly, the setup investment pays back quickly.

Custom GPT design principles:

  • One GPT, one job. A GPT that does five different report types is fragile. A GPT that produces exactly one report reliably is a tool you'll trust.
  • Explicit knowledge. Upload the documents, style guides, and templates your GPT needs rather than describing them in instructions alone.
  • Clear output format. Define exactly what the output should look like before you configure the GPT. The output format is the contract between the GPT and whoever uses it.
  • Graceful failure handling. Write instructions for what the GPT should do when it encounters something outside its scope — ask for clarification rather than hallucinating an answer.

What makes a personal Custom GPT into a team tool:

The key is separating configuration from logic. Your proposal GPT knows the steps: intake the brief, identify the key asks, structure the response, apply your format. The configuration — your specific templates, your client segments, your pricing ranges — is loaded as knowledge files that teammates can swap out for their own.

When the logic and configuration are mixed into one long instruction block, sharing the GPT means your teammate inherits your specific setup. Every teammate has to edit the system prompt to match their context. That's not a team tool — it's a personal GPT with extra steps.

When the logic lives in system instructions and the configuration lives in uploaded files, your teammate duplicates the GPT, replaces the knowledge files with their own documents, and has a working tool tailored to their context. The GPT is genuinely portable.

The documentation question: GPTs you build can be shared publicly in the GPT Store or privately within your team workspace. When you build something that saves you 15 minutes per use and you do that workflow 5 times a week, you're looking at over an hour per week in savings. If your teammate has the same workflow, sharing it doubles that impact. The documentation isn't overhead — it's what makes the savings transferable.

What good Custom GPT documentation includes:

  • What it does (one sentence)
  • When to use it (specific trigger situations)
  • What knowledge files need to be uploaded and in what format
  • Step-by-step setup instructions for new users
  • One or two usage examples with expected output

The sharing moment: The best time to write documentation is immediately after you've used the GPT three times. At that point you know the edge cases, you've seen what confuses new users, and the workflow is fresh enough to explain clearly. GPTs documented after three uses get shared. GPTs documented six months later stay personal.

Versioning your Custom GPTs: GPTs evolve. Your proposal GPT from six months ago might not handle your current service offerings. Keep a changelog in the GPT's description — even just one line per change — so teammates know when the GPT last updated and what changed. A stale GPT that looks current is worse than one clearly labeled as last updated six months ago.

The economics of Custom GPT building — how to think about the investment:

Every Custom GPT you build has an ROI. The math is simple: time to build divided by time saved per use, measured in how many uses it takes to break even. A GPT that takes 2 hours to build and saves you 10 minutes per use pays back after 12 uses. If you use it twice a week, that's 6 weeks to break even — then pure savings forever after.

The ROI calculation changes based on whether you share the GPT. A GPT shared with 5 teammates multiplies the savings by 5. That 6-week break-even becomes a little over 1 week. This is why documentation matters: a well-documented, shareable Custom GPT has dramatically better economics than a personal shortcut you keep to yourself.

When to build vs. when to install from the GPT Store: Before spending 2 hours building a Custom GPT, spend 15 minutes searching the GPT Store. If a marketplace GPT handles 80% of your use case, install it and use Custom Instructions for the remaining 20% rather than building from scratch. The custom-built path makes sense when: the workflow is specific to your knowledge base or process, the GPT Store option requires sharing data you'd prefer to keep internal, or the time savings are large enough that build-vs-buy math clearly favors building.

GPT Store discovery — finding what already exists: The GPT Store grows constantly. A GPT that didn't exist when you built your custom tool six months ago might exist now and be better than what you built. Schedule a quarterly GPT Store scan alongside your quarterly skill audit. Search your top three workflows. If a Store GPT now covers what you built, compare quality. If your custom version is better, consider submitting it to the Store — your personal shortcut could become a community resource.

The team GPT library: If you work with a team, the most leveraged thing you can do is create a shared library of Custom GPTs — version-controlled configurations and knowledge files maintained together. The library compounds: each person's most valuable workflow becomes available to everyone. A team of five people, each contributing two high-quality Custom GPTs, has a library of ten GPTs available to all five. That's ten times the individual investment with immediate collective returns.

Skills work differently at each tier: Custom Instructions for behavior, GPT Store installs for structured workflows, GPT Builder for custom automation. Build Custom GPTs when a repeated workflow isn't covered by any Store option. Design principles: one GPT, one job, explicit knowledge files, clear output format, graceful failure. Calculate ROI before building: time to build divided by time saved per use. Sharing multiplies the ROI. The key to shareability is separating configuration (files) from logic (instructions). Write documentation after three uses when edge cases are fresh. Version your GPTs so teammates know what's current. Check the GPT Store quarterly before building something custom. Build a team GPT library — each person's best workflow becomes everyone's tool.

exercises.prompt: You need ChatGPT to follow your team's PR review process. Which approach gives you the best balance of setup effort and long-term value?
options: Custom Instructions -- quick setup, works immediately, but you paste code manually each time
A Custom GPT built in GPT Builder -- reads uploaded code standards, runs structured checks, formatted output every time
No skill needed -- just describe the review process in each conversation
Both A and B are equal -- pick whichever is more convenient
exercises.explanation: For a daily, repeated workflow like PR reviews, a Custom GPT is the investment that pays off. It has your coding standards loaded as knowledge, runs structured checks, and produces consistent output. Custom Instructions work for occasional use but don't scale for 10 reviews a day.
hints: Think about the tradeoff: setup time vs. per-use time savings over 10 reviews a day.

exercises.prompt: You've built a useful proposal Custom GPT. A colleague wants to use it too. Write a prompt to ChatGPT that creates documentation and configuration options so the GPT is shareable.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You built a Custom GPT that writes client proposals: intake brief, identify key asks, structure response, apply your template. It saves you 20 minutes per proposal. Your colleague wants it but has different templates and client segments. Write a prompt to ChatGPT that creates shareable documentation.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["README","documentation","doc","instructions","setup","guide"],"label":"Asked for documentation","points":1},{"keyword_group":["config","configuration","configurable","variable","setting","customize","knowledge file","upload"],"label":"Mentioned configuration via knowledge files","points":2},{"keyword_group":["template","proposal","format","structure","step"],"label":"Referenced the workflow steps","points":1},{"keyword_group":["example","usage","demo","sample"],"label":"Asked for usage examples","points":1},{"keyword_group":["duplicate","copy","GPT Builder","share","teammate"],"label":"Addressed sharing/duplication","points":2}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Create a README for my Proposal GPT that includes: 1) What it does (intake brief, structure response, apply template), 2) Knowledge files to upload (proposal template, service catalog, pricing guide) so colleagues swap their own files, 3) Setup instructions in GPT Builder, 4) Example prompts to use it, 5) What to customize vs. leave as-is. Make it so a colleague can duplicate the GPT, swap the knowledge files, and be up and running in 30 minutes.","tips":["Separating knowledge files from instructions is what makes a personal GPT into a team tool.","Real usage examples help colleagues understand what to expect.","A clear file list means they adapt content, not rewrite logic."]}
exercises.explanation: A well-packaged Custom GPT separates knowledge files from system instructions. The instructions handle the workflow; the files handle the context-specific content. Documentation with examples turns personal shortcuts into team tools.
hints: Think about what makes a tool usable by someone who didn't build it.

lessons.title: ChatGPT Memory & Persistent Context
lessons.description: How to use ChatGPT Memory, Custom GPT knowledge, and conversation history to eliminate re-explaining yourself every session.

sections.content:
Most people use ChatGPT like it has amnesia. Every new conversation starts from scratch. You paste in the background, explain the project, re-establish the tone, and re-specify all the constraints — before you even get to the actual work. This is the re-explain tax, and it's one of the most significant sources of friction in most ChatGPT workflows.

Context is the difference between a smart assistant and a smart assistant who knows you.

A mediocre prompt with rich context produces better results than a perfect prompt with no context. The model is the same. The context is what changes the output quality. ChatGPT's memory features exist specifically to eliminate the re-explain tax — you set up context once, and it persists across conversations.

Three ways ChatGPT maintains context:

  • ChatGPT Memory — when enabled in Settings → Personalization, ChatGPT remembers facts about you across conversations. Your role, preferences, ongoing projects. You can view, edit, or delete memories anytime.
  • Custom Instructions — persistent instructions that shape every response. Your role, preferred format, what to always or never do. Set once, applied everywhere.
  • Custom GPT knowledge files — documents, templates, and references you upload to a Custom GPT. Every conversation in that GPT starts with your files already loaded.

One-time configuration, permanent benefit. Every future conversation starts fully informed.

The re-explain tax in concrete terms: Think about the last time you started a ChatGPT conversation about an ongoing project. How much time did you spend on setup before you got to actual work? Five minutes? Ten? For professionals who use ChatGPT multiple times a day on the same projects, that tax compounds fast. A consultant who runs five ChatGPT conversations per day and spends five minutes on setup each time is spending 25 minutes daily just re-explaining. That's over two hours per week, or 100 hours per year — gone before any real work starts. A well-configured memory system eliminates that entirely.

What makes a great Custom Instructions setup: The goal is not to write a paragraph about yourself. The goal is to give ChatGPT every piece of information it would need to handle any task without asking clarifying questions. That means: who you are, who your audience is, what good output looks like, what the constraints are, and what you've already decided. Think of it as writing a briefing for someone starting your job on Monday. What do they need to know to not embarrass themselves or you on the first day?

Getting the most from ChatGPT Memory:

ChatGPT Memory works best when you actively curate it rather than letting it accumulate passively. You can tell ChatGPT what to remember: "Remember that I prefer responses in bullet points" or "Remember that my main client is in healthcare and uses Epic." You can also view your memory list in Settings → Personalization → Manage Memory and remove anything outdated or incorrect.

The key insight: Memory is a tool you control, not a black box. Check it monthly to remove stale facts. Add new context explicitly when your situation changes. A well-maintained memory list acts like a briefing document that auto-applies to every conversation.

Using Custom GPTs as persistent context containers:

For project-specific work, a Custom GPT is even more powerful than Memory. Upload your project brief, your style guide, your templates, your reference documents. Every conversation in that GPT starts with all of those loaded. You're not re-uploading — you're just having the conversation.

This is especially valuable for:

  • Client work — one Custom GPT per client, loaded with their docs, style, and history
  • Content creation — your voice guide, audience notes, and past examples always available
  • Research projects — source documents and notes loaded for the duration of the project
  • Team standards — shared GPT loaded with your team's processes, templates, and guidelines

Template for writing effective Custom GPT instructions: "You are [role]. You help me with [specific tasks]. Always [key behaviors]. Never [constraints]. When answering, [format preferences]. The documents in your knowledge include [what's uploaded and when to use each]." Explicit instructions about what the knowledge files contain and when to use them produce better results than assuming ChatGPT will figure out the right usage pattern.

Context compounds — and goes stale:

One of the less obvious benefits of rich persistent context is that it accumulates value over time. Today ChatGPT knows your project. Next month it knows your project plus the preferences you've explicitly told it to remember, plus the knowledge files you've kept updated, plus the patterns it's learned from your Custom Instructions. The system gets more useful in proportion to how much context you give it.

But context also goes stale. A Custom GPT set up six months ago might have pricing that's changed, clients that have churned, templates that are outdated. Stale context is worse than no context — it confidently gives you answers based on information that's no longer true.

The maintenance habit: Schedule quarterly reviews of your persistent context. Fifteen minutes, four times a year. Review your Memory list, your Custom Instructions, and every uploaded knowledge file in your active Custom GPTs. Update what's changed. Remove what's outdated. A well-maintained context system with 90% accurate information is dramatically more valuable than a neglected one with 70% stale data.

The types of context that go stale fastest: Pricing and rates change. Client relationships evolve. Team members join and leave. Goals shift from quarter to quarter. These are the things to check first in every quarterly review.

The types of context that stay accurate longest: Your professional background and expertise don't change quickly. Communication style is relatively stable. Standard templates and formats have long shelf lives. Core constraints (things you never do, privacy requirements, brand rules) are usually durable. Spend less time reviewing these and more time on the volatile categories.

A practical audit prompt: Start each quarterly review by asking ChatGPT: "Based on what I've told you to remember and the documents in this GPT, what looks like it might be outdated or contradicts recent information I've given you? List the top things to review." ChatGPT will surface the most obvious inconsistencies before you do a full manual review. That takes the quarterly review from 45 minutes to 15.

Persistent context eliminates the re-explain tax — which costs 100+ hours per year for regular users. Use ChatGPT Memory for personal preferences, Custom Instructions for behavior rules, and Custom GPT knowledge files for project-specific documents. Actively curate your Memory list — check it monthly, add new context explicitly, remove stale facts. Write Custom GPT instructions that tell ChatGPT what each knowledge file contains and when to use it. Context compounds over time — the more you give the system, the more useful it becomes. But context also goes stale — schedule quarterly reviews. A well-maintained context system is what separates users who get occasional help from users who have a genuine AI operating system.

exercises.prompt: You're a management consultant using ChatGPT for your business with 5 clients. Write Custom Instructions that eliminate re-explaining yourself every session.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You're a management consultant with 5 active clients. You write proposals weekly and have a specific communication style. Currently every ChatGPT conversation starts from scratch. Write Custom Instructions that set up persistent context.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["role","consultant","management","consulting","who I am"],"label":"Defined professional role","points":1},{"keyword_group":["client","account","5","five","list"],"label":"Referenced client portfolio","points":1},{"keyword_group":["style","tone","voice","communication","direct","data-driven"],"label":"Specified communication style","points":2},{"keyword_group":["format","bullet","structured","output","response"],"label":"Specified output format preferences","points":1},{"keyword_group":["avoid","never","don't","constraint","always"],"label":"Included dos and don'ts","points":1},{"keyword_group":["custom instruction","persist","always","every conversation","assume"],"label":"Written as persistent instructions","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"I'm a management consultant. My clients include [5 client names with one-line context each]. My communication style is direct and data-driven -- I use concrete numbers and avoid jargon. Always structure responses with clear headers. Never use filler language. Assume I have business context; don't explain basics. When I mention a client by name, assume we have an ongoing engagement. For proposals, follow executive summary → problem → approach → outcomes → pricing structure.","tips":["Include your communication style as explicit dos and don'ts, not just adjectives.","Client context should include relationship status and current priorities.","Explicit constraints (never, always) give ChatGPT clearer guardrails than soft guidance."]}
exercises.explanation: Custom Instructions with persistent context eliminate repetitive setup. One-time configuration (role, clients, style, format) makes every future conversation immediately productive.
hints: What do you re-explain most often? That's what goes in Custom Instructions first.

exercises.prompt: Match each ChatGPT context method to what it's best used for. Knowing the right tool prevents you from stuffing everything into Custom Instructions.
exercises.correct_answer: {"ChatGPT Memory":"Persistent personal facts across all conversations -- your role, preferences, and ongoing projects that ChatGPT should always know","Custom Instructions":"Behavior rules and output format preferences that apply to every ChatGPT response, everywhere","Custom GPT knowledge files":"Project-specific documents, templates, and references that a dedicated GPT needs to do its job","Conversation history":"Context from earlier in the same conversation -- no setup needed, ChatGPT reads the thread","File uploads (per conversation)":"One-time documents for a specific task -- when you need to analyze something without building a permanent GPT","Custom actions":"Live connections to external APIs and tools -- for when ChatGPT needs to read or write real-time data"}
exercises.explanation: Each context method has a different scope and cost. Using the right one means your persistent context stays lean and your per-conversation work stays minimal.
hints: Think about scope: is this fact needed everywhere, in one project, or just this conversation?

exercises.prompt: Your Custom GPT context keeps going stale. Walk through keeping it current.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Keeping Context Fresh", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You set up your Consulting Custom GPT two weeks ago. It works well but you just realized ChatGPT still references old pricing that changed last Tuesday. Your knowledge file is stale."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "After two weeks, your pricing changed, one client churned, and you onboarded a new client. The Custom GPT doesn't know about any of this.", "question": "How do you fix the stale context?", "options": ["Mention the updates in the next conversation and hope ChatGPT remembers", "Update the knowledge file and re-upload it to the Custom GPT configuration", "Create a new Custom GPT from scratch with current information"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Update the knowledge file and re-upload. In-conversation mentions don't persist to future sessions unless ChatGPT Memory captures them. A new GPT loses your accumulated instructions. Targeted file updates keep the good and fix the stale."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "Your pricing doc and client list are updated. But after a month, you notice ChatGPT sometimes uses outdated strategy information from your original Q1 goals doc.", "question": "How do you prevent context staleness as a system?", "options": ["Review and update Custom GPT knowledge files quarterly -- set a calendar reminder", "Delete all old docs after each quarter", "Don't worry about it -- ChatGPT handles outdated info fine"], "correct": 0, "feedback": "Scheduled reviews prevent staleness. Quarterly reminder: update knowledge files, remove outdated uploads, refresh Custom Instructions. Deleting old docs without replacing loses your baseline. Ignoring staleness means increasingly wrong answers."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "It's time for your quarterly context review. Write a prompt that asks ChatGPT to help you identify what in your Custom GPT might be outdated.", "placeholder": "Write your context audit prompt...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["review", "audit", "check", "evaluate", "assess"], "label": "Requested a review", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["outdated", "stale", "old", "current", "accurate", "up to date"], "label": "Referenced freshness", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["client", "pricing", "goal", "strategy", "doc", "document", "knowledge"], "label": "Named specific context areas to check", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["update", "change", "remove", "add", "replace"], "label": "Asked for specific update recommendations", "points": 1}], "ideal": "Review the context you have about me and my work. Based on what I've told you recently in this conversation, flag anything in your knowledge that looks outdated or contradictory. Specifically check: my client list (any changes?), pricing (current?), Q2 goals (still accurate?), my templates (still the format I use?). For each issue, tell me what to update in the knowledge file.", "max_points": 6, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Context compounds but also goes stale. Scheduled reviews (quarterly), targeted file updates (not rebuilds), and audit prompts keep your Custom GPT accurate over time.
hints: What information changes most often in your work? That's what needs the most frequent updates.

lessons.title: Chain Tasks Into Workflows
lessons.description: Build sequences where one output feeds the next -- from meeting notes to client prep kits.

sections.content:
Single prompts are powerful. Chained prompts are transformative.

The difference: a single prompt asks ChatGPT to do one thing. A chained workflow is a sequence where step one's output automatically becomes step two's input, and step two's output feeds step three. No manual handoff. No copy-pasting results from one prompt to another. Just a pipeline that takes raw input at one end and produces a polished deliverable at the other.

A simple example: "Summarize this 40-page document" → "Extract the key decisions from that summary" → "Draft a follow-up email with each decision as an action item." Three prompts, but ChatGPT carries the context through each step. You don't re-paste the summary into the second prompt. The chain does it automatically.

A more complex example: meeting notes → extract action items with owners and deadlines → group by team member → draft individual Slack messages to each person with their action items. The final output is a set of personalized Slack messages, ready to send. The intermediate steps (extraction, grouping) happen without you managing them.

Why chains are more powerful than mega-prompts: When you ask ChatGPT to do too many things in one prompt, quality degrades. The chain approach keeps each step focused on one specific job, which means each step's output is as good as it can be before it becomes the input for the next step. Think assembly line, not one person doing everything at once.

The mental model for designing chains: Start from the output and work backwards. What is the finished deliverable? What's the last step that produces it? What does that step need as input? What step produces that input? Keep working backwards until you reach raw data. That reverse-engineering process reveals the exact chain structure, and it's much more reliable than trying to design forward from raw data to finished deliverable.

A real chain prompt template:

  • "Step 1: [specific job]. Output: [format]"
  • "Step 2: Take the output of Step 1 and [specific job]. Output: [format]"
  • "Step 3: Take the output of Step 2 and [specific job]. Final output: [format ready to use]"

Copy this structure and fill it in for any multi-step workflow. The explicit output format at each step is what prevents the handoff failures that break chains.

When to write the chain as one prompt vs. multiple prompts: For simple three-step chains, a single prompt with numbered steps works well. ChatGPT holds the context from step to step and runs through the chain in one response. For more complex chains — six or more steps, or chains where step 3 might branch into different paths — consider breaking into separate prompts, with each prompt picking up explicitly from the last. "Here's the output from step 2. Now run step 3." The slightly higher manual overhead is worth it for complex chains where a single-prompt approach tends to compress steps and lose detail.

The key to good chains: keep each step's job specific and narrow.

The most common chain design mistake is giving a step too much to do. "Summarize the document AND extract decisions AND draft follow-up emails" in one step means ChatGPT splits its attention across three different tasks and does each one at 70% instead of 100%. Break that into three steps. Each one gets 100% focus.

Think of it like an assembly line: each station does exactly one thing. The car doesn't get welded and painted at the same station. The weld quality is better when it's all one station does. Same principle applies to ChatGPT chains.

Diagnosing a broken chain: When a chain produces bad output, the problem is almost never the step itself — it's the handoff. The output of step N doesn't contain what step N+1 needs. Before blaming step 3, look at step 2's output. Is it giving step 3 the right information in the right format? Fix the handoff, not the step.

When to use a chain vs a single prompt:

  • Use a chain when the task has distinct stages, when the first stage's output needs to be processed differently for different paths, or when one step needs to make decisions that affect how the next step runs.
  • Use a single prompt when the task is genuinely atomic — one job, one output, no intermediate decisions required.

The branching chain: More advanced chains don't just run linearly — they branch. Step 2 might look at the output of Step 1 and take a different path depending on what it finds. Example: Step 1 extracts all feedback from customer interviews. Step 2 classifies each piece as feature request, bug report, or general sentiment. Step 3a handles feature requests. Step 3b handles bug reports. Step 3c handles sentiment. The branching is defined in Step 2's instructions: "For each feedback item, classify it AND append a routing label: [FEATURE], [BUG], or [SENTIMENT]. Step 3 processes will filter by these labels." Branches are powerful but add complexity — don't branch until linear chains are working reliably.

Chain outputs as assets: Well-designed chains produce outputs at every step that have value in their own right. The extracted action items from Step 2 of your meeting follow-up chain? Those might also be useful as a reference list separate from the Slack messages. Design your intermediate outputs to be coherent and useful, not just internal pass-through data. When intermediate outputs have standalone value, your chain becomes a richer production system.

Real workflows that power users actually build:

Morning Briefing: Paste today's calendar events and top emails → ChatGPT generates a 3-paragraph brief with priorities for the day, meetings to prep for, and decisions needed. Runs before you open your inbox. You start the day knowing exactly where to focus.

Meeting Follow-Up Machine: Paste meeting notes → extract action items with owners and deadlines → draft individual emails to each person with their items → generate a summary to update your project tracker. What used to take 45 minutes happens in 3.

Client Prep Kit: Before every client call, paste account history and recent email threads → ChatGPT generates a one-page brief with current status, open questions, and recommended talking points. You walk into every call knowing the full picture.

Content Repurposing Pipeline: Long-form article → extract 5 key insights → reformat each insight as a LinkedIn post in your voice → reformat the top insight as a Twitter/X thread → generate 3 email newsletter section options. One piece of content becomes six, each optimized for its platform.

Research Synthesis Engine: Paste 10 sources → extract relevant quotes and data points from each → identify themes and contradictions across sources → generate a structured synthesis with citations → draft an executive summary for non-technical stakeholders. Research that used to take a day takes an hour.

The common thread: ChatGPT handles gathering and organizing. You handle thinking and deciding. The chain removes the gathering friction so your mental energy goes to the work that actually requires judgment.

Chaining means each step's output feeds the next automatically — no manual handoff required. Design chains by working backwards from the finished output. Keep each step focused on one specific job. When a chain breaks, diagnose the handoff between steps, not the steps themselves. Use branching chains for workflows that take different paths based on intermediate results. Build chains for multi-stage workflows where gathering and organizing are the bottleneck. Real examples: morning briefings, meeting follow-ups, client prep kits, content repurposing, research synthesis. The chain handles the assembly; you provide the judgment.

exercises.prompt: You built a 5-step chain but step 3 keeps producing bad output. What's the most likely problem?
options: The AI model isn't smart enough for step 3
Step 2's output doesn't contain the information step 3 needs -- the handoff is broken
You need to combine steps 2 and 3 into one mega-step
Start the entire chain over with different data
exercises.explanation: When a chain step fails, the most common cause is a broken handoff: the previous step's output doesn't contain what the current step needs. Check the output of step 2 -- is it giving step 3 the right inputs? Fix the handoff, not the step.
hints: Think about an assembly line. If station 3 is producing bad work, where do you look first?

exercises.prompt: A product manager needs to go from raw customer feedback to a prioritized feature list. Write a prompt that designs a multi-step chain for this.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You have 50 raw customer feedback entries from support tickets, surveys, and sales calls. You need a prioritized feature recommendation. Write a prompt that chains the steps from raw data to polished output.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["categorize","classify","theme","group","sort"],"label":"Included categorization step","points":1},{"keyword_group":["count","frequency","rank","volume","how many","most"],"label":"Mentioned frequency analysis","points":1},{"keyword_group":["roadmap","current","existing","planned","compare","cross-reference"],"label":"Referenced roadmap comparison","points":2},{"keyword_group":["gap","missing","not on","unaddressed","new"],"label":"Identified gaps","points":1},{"keyword_group":["prioritize","recommend","rank","top","evidence","data"],"label":"Produced prioritized recommendations","points":2}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Chain: Step 1) Read all 50 feedback entries and categorize by theme. Step 2) Count frequency of each theme and rank by volume. Step 3) Cross-reference top themes against our current product roadmap. Step 4) Identify gaps -- high-demand themes not on the roadmap. Step 5) Draft a prioritized feature recommendation with supporting evidence from the feedback.","tips":["Each step has one clear job. Don't combine categorizing and ranking.","The cross-reference step is what turns frequency data into actionable insight.","Evidence from the original feedback makes recommendations credible."]}
exercises.explanation: A well-designed chain: categorize, count, compare, identify gaps, recommend. Each step has one job and its output feeds the next.
hints: Start with raw data, work toward a polished recommendation. What happens at each stage?

exercises.prompt: Your meeting follow-up chain consistently gets action item owners wrong because your notes don't specify who volunteered. Write a prompt that fixes the input problem.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"Your automated meeting follow-up system has been running for a week. It drafts great emails and extracts action items, but it keeps assigning tasks to the wrong people because your notes don't clearly indicate who volunteered for what. Fix the system.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["action","task","item","assignment","owner"],"label":"Referenced action items","points":1},{"keyword_group":["person","name","who","owner","volunteer","responsible"],"label":"Addressed ownership attribution","points":2},{"keyword_group":["format","structure","template","pattern","convention"],"label":"Defined a structured format","points":2},{"keyword_group":["date","deadline","by","due","when"],"label":"Included deadlines","points":1},{"keyword_group":["note","input","capture","write","record"],"label":"Fixed the note-taking approach","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"I'm updating my note-taking format to fix action item attribution. From now on, I'll write action items as: 'ACTION: [Person] will [task] by [date]'. When processing meeting notes, look for this pattern first. If an action item doesn't have a clear owner, flag it as 'UNASSIGNED' instead of guessing. For the follow-up email, list unassigned items separately so I can assign them before sending.","tips":["When automation produces consistent errors, fix the input -- not just the processing.","A structured format (ACTION: person will task by date) is machine-readable.","Flagging unknowns as 'UNASSIGNED' is safer than guessing."]}
exercises.explanation: When automation has recurring errors, fix the input. Your note-taking is part of the system -- a structured format for action items makes the whole pipeline reliable.
hints: The system is only as good as its inputs. Where is the data quality problem?

lessons.title: Automate With Guardrails
lessons.description: Triggers, schedules, and automation boundaries -- when to let ChatGPT run and when to keep hands on the wheel.

sections.content:
Making chains run without you is the next level. Automation means ChatGPT executes on a trigger or schedule: morning briefing at 7am, inbox triaged every hour, weekly report at Friday 4pm. The work runs whether you think about it or not.

But automation without guardrails is a liability, not an asset. The rule that prevents most problems: automate the gathering and drafting. Keep humans on the approving and sending.

What this looks like in practice: your morning briefing automation runs at 7am and drops a document in your inbox with priorities, urgent emails, and meeting prep. You read it. You decide what to act on. Nothing went out. Nothing was changed. The automation did the gathering; you did the deciding.

Compare to an automation that sends weekly client status emails automatically without review. It works great for 11 weeks. In week 12, it sends the wrong template to the wrong segment because a data source had an unexpected format change. The emails are out. You can't un-send them. The approval gate that would have caught this in 60 seconds of review is the difference between a recoverable mistake and a client relationship problem.

The golden rule of automation: The only thing that should happen without human review is gathering and organizing information. The moment automation reaches out to another person — sends an email, posts to Slack, submits a form, makes a purchase — a human needs to be in the loop before it happens.

What to include in every automation design:

  • A clear definition of what the automation does and does not do
  • An approval gate before any external action
  • A logging mechanism that records every action taken
  • An exception path — what happens when the automation encounters something unexpected
  • A kill switch — how to pause or stop the automation immediately if something goes wrong

Automation without these five elements is not production automation. It's a script waiting to cause a problem.

The trust ladder: how to expand automation responsibly:

Start with automation that saves you time on prep work — gathering data, generating first drafts, organizing information. As you build confidence that the output is consistently high quality, gradually expand what the automation handles.

Level 1 — Safe to automate immediately: Repetitive, predictable inputs. Low stakes if wrong. Output is reviewed before any action. Internal reports, draft emails that sit in your drafts folder, data pulls, summaries.

Level 2 — Automate with review gate: External-facing but reviewable before send. Client reports, Slack announcements to your team, status updates that go through an approval step. The automation does the drafting; a human approves before delivery.

Level 3 — Keep human in the loop throughout: Requires nuanced judgment. High consequences if wrong. Client negotiations, hiring decisions, financial commitments, anything involving ambiguity that requires your experience and context to navigate.

Never jump directly to Level 1 for high-stakes work. Prove it works at a lower level first. An automation that's been running correctly for 3 months internally might be ready for external-facing use. An automation you just built is not.

Shadow mode — the bridge between levels: When you're ready to expand an automation from Level 1 to Level 2, don't flip the switch immediately. Run in shadow mode first: the automation performs the external action but also sends you a copy so you can review what went out. Run shadow mode for two to four weeks before fully handing off. This lets you verify quality in the real environment without trusting blindly. Any issues you catch in shadow mode are fixable. Issues you catch after the automation has been running without review for months are often expensive to remediate.

Thinking architecturally about where ChatGPT fits in your workflow:

The top 1% of ChatGPT users don't just use it for individual tasks. They look at their entire workflow and ask: where is ChatGPT's role in this system? They design for integration, not isolation. Their ChatGPT setup is a component in a larger operating system, not a standalone tool they reach for occasionally.

What architectural thinking looks like in practice: map your weekly recurring work. Identify every task where you spend time gathering, organizing, or formatting information that already exists somewhere. Those are your automation candidates. Build a chain for the highest-value one. Run it for a month. When the output is consistently good, either expand it or build the next one.

The exception library: Every automation will eventually encounter something it doesn't know how to handle. A file in an unexpected format. A client who isn't in your notes. A meeting with no agenda. The sophisticated approach is to build an exception library: a running list of every exception the automation has encountered and how it was resolved. Over time, this library becomes the basis for expanding the automation's coverage. Today's exception is tomorrow's handled case.

Building the maintenance habit: Automations require maintenance. Not much — but some. A monthly five-minute check: did the automation run correctly last month? Any new exceptions? Any changes in the upstream data sources that might have affected output quality? This habit prevents automation drift — the gradual degradation of output quality that happens when automations run unchecked against changing data environments.

Your next step: Pick your single most time-consuming recurring workflow. Map it. Chain it. Automate what makes sense at Level 1. Run it for a month. When the output is consistently good, consider Level 2 expansion. That one workflow, fully optimized, will save more time than a hundred clever one-off prompts. The compounding value of a good automation is weeks of time per year. That's what makes the upfront investment worthwhile.

The error budget mindset: Perfect automation is a fantasy. Real automations make errors. The question isn't whether your automation will ever produce wrong output — it will. The question is: what's the acceptable error rate, and what happens when an error occurs? Define this before deploying. For an internal report, an error rate of 1-2% is probably fine — a reviewer will catch it. For a client-facing output, your tolerance might be near zero. For anything irreversible, the tolerance should be zero and the only acceptable approach is human review before action. Knowing your error budget lets you design the right guardrails instead of either over-engineering or under-protecting.

Building feedback loops into automations: The best automations get smarter over time because they have built-in feedback mechanisms. The simplest form: after each automated output, make it easy to flag something as wrong. A thumbs-up/thumbs-down in a Slack message. A "flag for review" option in an email. A dedicated #automation-issues channel where problems get logged. Collect this feedback and use it to refine your prompts and rules monthly. An automation with a feedback loop converges toward reliable. An automation without feedback loops drifts.

Automate gathering and drafting. Keep humans on approving and sending. Every automation needs five elements: clear scope, approval gate, logging, exception path, kill switch. Climb the trust ladder gradually — internal before external, proven before expanded. Use shadow mode as the bridge between automation levels. Design architecturally: look at your whole workflow and identify where automation removes gathering friction. Build an exception library to expand coverage over time. Do monthly maintenance checks to prevent drift. One fully optimized workflow saves more time than a hundred clever one-off prompts. The compounding value is months of time per year.

exercises.prompt: Your automated weekly report has been running perfectly for 3 months. Your boss asks if it can also send directly to the board. What do you say?
options: Sure -- it's been reliable for 3 months, it can handle board distribution too
Add the board to the distribution but keep human review before sending -- board communications are higher stakes than internal team reports
Board reports need a completely different system
Sure, but add a disclaimer that it's AI-generated
exercises.explanation: Same report, higher audience = higher stakes. A report that's fine for your team might need polish before going to the board. Add board distribution but keep human review. The trust ladder: reliable for internal use doesn't automatically mean ready for executive distribution.
hints: The report hasn't changed, but the audience has. What changes about the risk profile?

exercises.prompt: You manage 5 workflows. Write a prompt to ChatGPT that classifies each workflow by automation level: fully automate, draft with human review, or keep manual.
exercises.correct_answer: {"scenario":"You have 5 recurring workflows: daily Slack standup summary, client proposal emails, weekly analytics dashboard, salary negotiation responses, and support ticket categorization. Write a prompt that helps ChatGPT classify each by the right automation level.","scoring_criteria":[{"keyword_group":["internal","low stakes","low risk","safe","team"],"label":"Identified internal/low-stakes criteria","points":2},{"keyword_group":["client","external","facing","customer","send"],"label":"Identified external-facing criteria","points":1},{"keyword_group":["review","human","approve","check","before sending"],"label":"Mentioned human review for medium-risk","points":2},{"keyword_group":["judgment","decision","sensitive","consequence","high stakes"],"label":"Identified high-stakes criteria","points":1},{"keyword_group":["categorize","classify","tier","level","bucket"],"label":"Used classification framework","points":1}],"max_points":7,"pass_threshold":4,"ideal_prompt":"Classify these 5 workflows by automation level. Criteria: FULLY AUTOMATE if internal, low stakes, and errors are easily caught. DRAFT + HUMAN REVIEW if external-facing or represents the company. KEEP MANUAL if requires nuanced judgment or has high consequences. Workflows: 1) Daily Slack standup summary, 2) Client proposal emails, 3) Weekly analytics dashboard, 4) Salary negotiation responses, 5) Support ticket categorization. For each, explain your reasoning.","tips":["Who sees the output determines the risk level.","Internal + repetitive = safe to fully automate.","Client-facing = always keep human review on the send."]}
exercises.explanation: Classification framework: internal + low stakes = fully automate, external-facing = draft with review, high-stakes judgment = keep manual. The audience and consequences determine the automation level.
hints: For each workflow, ask: who sees it and what happens if it's wrong?

exercises.prompt: Design automation boundaries for a complete client reporting system, then optimize it after a month of running.
exercises.correct_answer: {"title": "Automation Boundaries", "steps": [{"type": "context", "text": "You manage 5 client accounts. Every Friday, each client gets a personalized status report. Currently takes 3 hours. You want to automate this but need to draw the right boundaries."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "The system has four phases: data gathering, summary writing, formatting, and sending. You need to decide where automation stops and human review starts.", "question": "Which phases should be fully automated vs. human-reviewed?", "options": ["Fully automate everything including sending -- it's just status reports", "Automate data gathering and drafting. Require human review before every send.", "Keep everything manual -- client communications are too important"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Data gathering and drafting are safe to automate. Client-facing sends always need a human scan. A 60-second review catches errors that take hours to fix."}, {"type": "choice", "claude_response": "After a month, reports ship with zero edits for 4 of 5 accounts. Account E still needs adjustments because the client's priorities shift frequently.", "question": "How do you handle the unpredictable account?", "options": ["Accept that Account E always needs manual adjustment", "Add a pre-report step: check Account E's latest email thread for priority changes before generating their report", "Remove Account E from the automated system entirely"], "correct": 1, "feedback": "Add a context-check step for E. Before generating E's report, paste latest communications to ChatGPT for priority shifts. The system adapts to dynamic clients by adding a perception step, not by giving up on automation."}, {"type": "prompt", "context": "After 3 months, your system handles 5 accounts in 20 minutes instead of 3 hours. Your colleague wants the same system for their 8 accounts. Write a prompt that helps them set up their version.", "placeholder": "Write your system handoff prompt...", "scoring_criteria": [{"keyword_group": ["framework", "process", "same structure", "adapt", "customize"], "label": "Referenced the reusable framework", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["account", "client", "specific", "their", "configure"], "label": "Mentioned per-account customization", "points": 2}, {"keyword_group": ["template", "data source", "pipeline", "step"], "label": "Defined the components to set up", "points": 1}, {"keyword_group": ["test", "iterate", "refine", "feedback"], "label": "Included testing and refinement", "points": 1}], "ideal": "Use the same framework for your 8 accounts: 1) Create a Custom GPT loaded with your report template and per-client context files. 2) Configure per-account rules: tone, metrics to include, priority contacts. 3) Run the pipeline with one test account first. 4) Refine based on what needs editing. 5) Roll out to all 8 once the first one ships with zero edits. Plan for a monthly review to encode recurring fixes into the Custom GPT knowledge.", "max_points": 6, "pass_threshold": 3}]}
exercises.explanation: Complete system: automate gathering and drafting, require human review before sends, add dynamic context for unpredictable accounts, and package the framework for reuse. That's top 1% thinking.
hints: Think full lifecycle: automate routine, flag exceptions, review before send, improve over time.
